Level_1_Needs_work: Tightly coupled routing; no clear ordering story; hard to add rules/providers.
Level_2_Getting_there: Some layering; ordering or config story incomplete.
Level_3_Acceptable: Clear rules/engine/providers split; documented rule ordering; extensible RoutingConfig usage.
Level_4_Outstanding: Level 3 plus policy clarity under change (new provider/rule) with minimal blast radius.

Level_1_Needs_work: Decisions wrong or arbitrary; fallback dishonest; risk not derivable from inputs.
Level_2_Getting_there: Partial correctness; edge cases weak; risk mapping thin.
Level_3_Acceptable: Decisions match declared predicates; honest fallback semantics; justified RiskLevel mapping.
Level_4_Outstanding: Level 3 plus comprehensive edge coverage and no silent default-first-provider behaviour.

Level_1_Needs_work: No tests for priority/tie-break/fallback; mvn test fails.
Level_2_Getting_there: Sparse tests; key routing paths untested.
Level_3_Acceptable: Tests encode priority, tie-break, and fallback; regressions fail loudly.
Level_4_Outstanding: Level 3 plus risk/rule matrix tests and regression harness for config changes.

Level_1_Needs_work: No traceable reasons; applied rules list unused; logs unusable under demo load.
Level_3_Acceptable: RouteDecision reasons and applied-rules lists trustworthy; DecisionLogger readable.
Level_4_Outstanding: Level 3 plus structured audit output suitable for support replay.

Level_1_Needs_work: No design note; scenarios not reproducible from submission.
Level_3_Acceptable: Design note with priority diagram/timeline; two example decision traces.
Level_4_Outstanding: Level 3 plus instructor can replay scenarios from documentation alone.
